title: Foundations: Soteriology – Justification
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: FOUNDATIONS - Lesson 10 - Soteriology_ - Justification - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Series Introduction and Review
subpoints: The Foundations series aims to restore root-level theology to its proper place
Review of previous lessons: effectual calling, regeneration, repentance unto life, faith in Jesus Christ
The three elements of saving faith: knowledge (notitia), assent (assensus), and trust (fiducia)
Saving faith is diametrically opposed to law-keeping

outline.title: The Nature of Justification
subpoints: Justification is a declarative act of God, not an infusion of righteousness
Westminster Confession Chapter 11: God freely justifies by pardoning sins and accounting persons as righteous
Nothing is done in or worked in us when God declares us righteous
To justify means to treat or declare as righteous regardless of the state of the person

outline.title: The Ground and Instrument of Justification
subpoints: The ground of justification is the perfect righteousness of Christ imputed to us through His blood
Imputation means to count, reckon, or credit righteousness to the believer
Faith is the only instrument by which justification is received
Abraham and David as examples of justification by faith across both sides of the Mosaic law

outline.title: The Timing and Continuance of Justification
subpoints: Justification is applied the moment faith is exercised at conversion
Justification continues and is never lost throughout the believer's life
God the Father always sees the believer as righteous through Christ
Though justified, believers may experience God's fatherly displeasure for sin and need repentance

outline.title: Justification Across the Testaments and Common Errors
subpoints: Old Testament saints were justified the same way as New Testament saints – by faith in the Messiah
Hebrews 11 confirms the same faith across the ages
Rejecting the concept of ‘final justification’ as Roman Catholic and New Perspective errors
True justification gives assurance and produces humility, not grounds for boasting

summary:
In this tenth lesson of the Foundations series, Pastor Tito Lyro teaches on the doctrine of justification as part of the broader ordo salutis (order of salvation). He begins with a review of previous lessons on repentance unto life and saving faith—noting that genuine faith has three elements: knowledge of the gospel, assent to its truth, and personal trust in Christ. He emphasizes that saving faith is entirely apart from law-keeping and is itself a gift of God.

The heart of the lesson is a careful exposition of justification as a declarative rather than transformative act. Drawing from Westminster Confession Chapter 11 and Romans 3, Pastor Lyro explains that when God justifies the sinner, nothing is infused or worked within the person—rather, God declares the believer righteous on the basis of Christ’s imputed obedience and atoning blood. The ground of justification is entirely Christ’s righteousness, and faith is the sole instrument by which that righteousness is received.

Pastor Lyro also addresses the historical continuity of justification by faith, using Paul’s examples of Abraham and David in Romans 4 to show that both pre- and post-Mosaic saints were saved in exactly the same way. Hebrews 11 confirms that the same faith characterized all Old Testament believers. He refutes the ‘final justification’ teaching found in certain Protestant circles and associated with Roman Catholic sacramentalism, insisting that justification is a historical event that, once experienced, continues permanently.

The lesson closes with a note that this doctrine should produce assurance of salvation—since it is all God’s work—and humility, since nothing in the believer prompted God to declare them righteous. Future lessons will address sanctification in both its definitive and progressive forms.
transcript:
## Introduction and Series Context

The rest of us continue on our series on foundations, foundations of the Christian faith. I've ever heard of the book of the Institutes of the Christian faith. It's a John Calvin wrote it. He first edition of published in 1536. The final edition was published in 1559. And throughout these years, he kept on reworking, changing order of chapters, trying to find the ideal way to teach. The Christian faith is interesting that today, those books are viewed as high theology. Only for those who are going to seminary and so on. And yet, the Institutes, the word institutes means the basic, the principles, the elemental principles of the Christian faith.

The prophets, to the larger and shorter catechisms, prepared by the Westminster Assembly. The prophet says that the shorter catechisms was written for children and those not intelligent enough to memorize the larger catechisms. So the adults were intended to memorize the larger catechisms. And now even the shorter catechisms view this something that is for the highly, theologically minded and so on. And we have moved some of the things that have historically considered foundations to the Christian faith and stuff that should be on the root level, to the leaf level, and have forgotten to study these things.

So that's one of the reasons why we're going through this series and we're approaching the end of a few more weeks and we should be done with this series. To bring some of the stuff that now is considered high, leaf theology back to where it needs to be to the root, the foundation of what we believe. We have studied what the Bible says concerning men, concerning humanity, concerning creation, concerning providence, concerning the Bible itself. And then we came into what the Bible talks about concerning salvation, the doctrine of salvation or soteriology. And we've been looking at it in the particular order, the order in which God has worked these things out logically, and we've been looking at the ordo salutis.

## Review of Previous Lessons

A bit of review from last week: last week we took a look at repentance unto life and faith in Jesus Christ. Remember, these are, this is a logical progression. I'm not saying that these things are happening necessarily chronologically, but as we think about them, it's helpful to think about them happening in this logical order.

We define repentance to life as that initial radical and conscious change of opinion, feeling and purpose with respect to God, ourselves, sin and righteousness. And we saw also last week that faith in Jesus consists in three elements, that when we put our faith in Christ, there are three things that we're doing. We know the gospel, you cannot come to faith in Jesus Christ by accident. You have to accept the gospel as true. And then you have to make that yours. Faith receives that as being ours. It's cognition passing to conviction, and trust is conviction passing to confidence.

And saving faith is the result of regeneration, because we have been receiving a new heart, we are able to believe. And lastly, we saw that saving faith is diametrically opposed to law keeping.

## The Nature of Justification

So, justification. That's a big, important issue that is regularly under attack in the Church of Jesus Christ. It seems like every generation faces an attack in this doctrine. The believer experiences justification, adoption, and sanctification in this life, after he has been effectually called and regenerated.

Our shorter catechism question 32 asks, what benefits do they that are effectually called partake of in this life? They are effectually called partake in this life of justification, adoption, and sanctification, and the several benefits which in this life do either accompany or flow from them.

In justification that happens in a moment. It's not a process. It's an act of God. It happens in a moment.

If you have your hymnal, turn to page 85 to find chapter 11 of the Westminster Confession of Faith. Look at paragraph one of chapter 11. Those whom God effectually calls he also freely justifies. And then the confession is going to go through a bunch of negations—he's going to tell us what justification is not, followed by some affirmations.

"He freely justifies—not by infusing righteousness into them… but by pardoning their sins and by accounting and accepting their persons as righteous. Not anything wrought in them or done by them, but for Christ's sake alone… not by imputing the act of believing… but by imputing the obedience and satisfaction of Christ unto them, they receiving and resting on him in his righteousness by faith, which faith they have not of themselves; it is a gift of God."

So that's what justification is. God saying, this person, I'm declaring this person to be righteous because they believe in Jesus Christ. And my Son is righteous. So I'm going to look at them through my Son. And in my Son, I'm going to see them as righteous. Nothing happened in us—all of it is outside of us.

## To Justify Means to Declare Righteous

So to justify means to treat as righteous or to declare as righteous regardless of the state of the person. The Bible uses it in just a few ways. You can justify God. In Psalm 51 the psalmist says, "That you might be found just when you speak and blameless when you judge." We can justify Christ—in 1 Timothy 3:16 it says that Christ was justified in the Spirit. And usually the term justify refers to God's justifying repentant sinners through faith in Jesus Christ.

So turn to Romans chapter 3. Look at verse 19: "Now we know that whatever the law says, it says to those who are under the law that every mouth may be stopped and all the world may become guilty before God. Therefore, by the deeds of the law, no flesh will be justified in his sight, for by the law is the knowledge of sin."

But now the righteousness of God, apart from the law is revealed—being witnessed by the law and the prophets—even the righteousness of God through faith in Jesus Christ to all and on all who believe. For all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God, being justified freely by His grace through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus.

Justification in the theological sense is God's declaring the repentant sinner to be righteous through faith in the Lord Jesus Christ. To justify does not mean to make righteous in practice. It means to declare to be righteous.

## The Ground of Justification

The ground of justification is not righteousness found in us. Look at Romans 4:5: "But to him who does not work, but believes on him who justifies the ungodly, his faith is accounted for righteousness." The ground of justification is the perfect righteousness of Christ applied to us on the basis of His blood shed for us. Look at Romans 5:9: "Much more then, having now been justified by His blood, we shall be saved from wrath through Him."

Faith is the only instrument to receive justification. The only thing that connects us to God is faith in the Lord Jesus Christ. In Romans 1:17: "For in it the righteousness of God is revealed from faith to faith; as it is written, 'The just shall live by faith.'" And in Romans 3:28: "Therefore we conclude that a man is justified by faith apart from the deeds of the law."

It's important that we remember that faith has always been the instrument of justification. When the very first person was ever saved by the Lord Jesus Christ, I believe it's Adam and Eve themselves—to the very last person—faith has always been that instrument.

## Justification Across the Testaments

The Westminster Confession in 11:6 says that the justification of believers under the Old Testament was in all these respects one and the same with the justification of believers under the New Testament.

Paul talks about Abraham and David in Romans 4, and he masterfully picked those two. Abraham lived before the law was given, and David lived after it was given. Both were saved by faith, not by keeping the law. Abraham believed in the Messiah to come, and Jesus confirms in John 8 that Abraham saw His day and rejoiced.

And if you had to go to another part of the New Testament that shows the Old Testament saints had the same faith, it would be Hebrews 11, where the Holy Spirit says "believe like these people did" and goes on listing a bunch of Old Testament saints.

## The Timing and Continuance of Justification

Justification is applied at the time faith is exercised. When we believe we are justified. You can see that in paragraph 4 of chapter 11. Justification continues—it's not like we're justified and then we're done being justified. We continue in a state of justification.

For the moment we believe in Jesus Christ, God the Father always sees us as righteous, as perfect, not guilty of our sins from that moment on. John tells us that in 1 John, where we continue to have access to God; we never stand apart from God.

Now as a believer, do you ever experience God feeling distant from you? Yes. So although the Christian is justified throughout life and all his sins are forgiven, he still may be chastened in this life, and even lose rewards in the next life for his sins. The Westminster Confession paragraph 5 says: "they may, by their sins, fall under God's fatherly displeasure, and not have the light of His countenance restored unto them until they humble themselves, confess their sins, beg pardon, and renew their faith and repentance."

## Objections: Final Justification

Lately it has been popular to talk about final justification, something that will happen at the end when Christ comes back. In some areas it's being talked about in Protestant Christianity in the sense that we won't know whether we made it to heaven or not till that final day. That's not what the Bible teaches. The Bible talks about this concept of final justification, but it's just a pronouncement of the acquittal that already happened in history.

Usually Protestant writers limit the term justification to that initial justification—not to some final process. Now as you think of this doctrine of justification, it should generate in us an assurance of salvation because it tells us that it all is the work of God. This doctrine also should cause humility in our hearts because it's not what God saw in us that caused Him to declare us to be His—it's what He saw in Jesus Christ.

## Conclusion

Any questions about the things that we've been talking about this morning? All right, so be here for the Kids Christmas program. And then we continue on this series in two weeks. The goal is to look at sanctification, both the definitive part—at the moment we come to faith, we are positionally perfect—and then we start a journey towards perfection. That's the definitive and progressive side of sanctification.

Father, having thank You that You are a God who saves, and we thank You that You saved through the Lord Jesus Christ. We pray that will be a reality for all of us. And that for those with us who do not believe in You, we pray that You would work in their hearts to come to faith in Jesus Christ. We ask this in Jesus' name. Amen.
